questions.text: Before collecting personal data, do you give individuals a clear notice covering: (a) what data is collected, (b) why it is needed, (c) their rights, and (d) who to contact?
questions.why: A valid Notice must cover all four elements. Consent given without a proper notice is not valid under the DPDP Act.

options.label: Yes — notice covers all four elements
Partially — notice is incomplete or unclear
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a separate and more stringent consent process for collecting data about children under 18 years of age?
questions.why: Children's data requires verifiable guardian consent, identification of minors, and stronger safeguards than adult data. If your programme does not work with under-18s, answer Yes.

options.label: Yes — or we do not work with under-18s
Partially — some safeguards exist but not fully in place
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Is consent obtained in a manner that is clear, specific, informed, and recorded — so you can demonstrate it was given?
questions.why: Verbal or implied consent is very difficult to defend. You must be able to produce a record if a beneficiary disputes consent.

options.label: Yes — written or digital record for all consents
Partially — some records exist but coverage is incomplete
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you collect only the personal data you actually need for your programme, avoiding extra details collected 'just in case'?
questions.why: Data Minimisation: only collect what is necessary for the stated purpose. Aadhaar, income, or caste should only be collected if directly required.

options.label: Yes — we collect only what is necessary
Partially — we sometimes collect more than needed
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a clear process for individuals to withdraw consent, and do you stop using their data promptly after withdrawal?
questions.why: Withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent. After withdrawal you must stop processing and delete the data unless another legal basis applies.

options.label: Yes — a clear process exists and is followed
Partially — withdrawal is possible but the process is unclear
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you know what personal data you hold, where it is stored, who can access it, and why you keep it?
questions.why: A data inventory — even a simple spreadsheet — is the foundation of all compliance. You cannot protect data you do not know exists.

options.label: Yes — a maintained data inventory exists
Partially — we have an informal sense but nothing documented
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Is personal data protected with reasonable security measures such as passwords, access controls, or encryption?
questions.why: Open WhatsApp groups or unprotected spreadsheets shared via 'anyone with the link' are a direct breach risk under the DPDP Act.

options.label: Yes — data is password-protected and access-controlled
Partially — some systems are protected, others are not
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do only the staff members who actually need the data have access to it?
questions.why: Access control is a core security safeguard. Not every staff member needs access to every beneficiary's personal data.

options.label: Yes — role-based access is in place
Partially — access is somewhat restricted but not formally managed
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a defined retention schedule stating how long each type of data is kept and when it is deleted?
questions.why: Data must be deleted once the purpose is complete. Keeping data indefinitely 'just in case' is not permitted under the Act.

options.label: Yes — a retention schedule exists and deletions are carried out
Partially — some retention rules exist but are not consistently followed
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a process — such as regular backups — ensuring personal data is not lost due to theft, damage, or system failure?
questions.why: Lost data cannot be recovered and may need to be reported as a breach. Security safeguards include ensuring data availability and integrity.

options.label: Yes — regular backups and a recovery process are in place
Partially — backups occur but are not tested or regular
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you use personal data only for the purpose you told the person about, unless another lawful basis applies?
questions.why: Purpose Limitation: data collected for one purpose cannot be used for another without fresh consent. Patient phone numbers must not be used for fundraising calls.

options.label: Yes — data is used only for its stated purpose
Partially — we sometimes use data beyond its original purpose
No / Not Sure

questions.text: If you share personal data with another organisation, do you clearly define why it is shared and how it will be protected?
questions.why: As the Data Fiduciary, you remain responsible for beneficiary data even when a partner or processor handles it. Written agreements are essential.

options.label: Yes — written data-sharing agreements exist with all partners
Partially — some agreements exist but coverage is incomplete
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do your staff avoid sharing beneficiary data via personal WhatsApp, personal email accounts, or other unsecured channels?
questions.why: Unsecured sharing is an avoidable breach risk. Reasonable security safeguards include controlling how data is transmitted.

options.label: Yes — staff use only official, secure channels for data
Partially — unofficial channels are sometimes used
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you know whether any vendor or online service (e.g. Google Forms, KoBoToolbox) stores data outside India, and have you assessed whether that arrangement is permitted?
questions.why: The government may impose conditions on cross-border transfers. NGOs should at minimum know where their data is hosted.

options.label: Yes — we know where all our data is hosted and have assessed the risk
Partially — we know about some platforms but not all
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Before using any new vendor, tool, or app that handles personal data, do you check whether it has adequate privacy and security practices?
questions.why: You are accountable for vendors who handle data on your behalf. Basic due diligence — checking for a privacy policy and encryption — is required.

options.label: Yes — vendor checks are done before adoption
Partially — some checks happen but not consistently
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a defined process for responding to requests to access personal data and information about how it is being used?
questions.why: Rights of access, correction, and erasure are legally enforceable. There must be a defined workflow — not a vague 'someone will handle it' approach.

options.label: Yes — a named person, log, and response timeline exist
Partially — requests are handled informally without a formal process
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Can individuals ask you to correct, complete, or update inaccurate personal data — and do you update it everywhere it appears?
questions.why: Right to Correction: inaccurate data can harm beneficiaries. The correction must flow to all records and partner systems where the data appears.

options.label: Yes — a correction process exists and is followed
Partially — corrections happen but not systematically
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Can individuals ask you to erase their personal data, and do you comply (subject to legal retention requirements)?
questions.why: Right to Erasure: when someone asks for deletion and there is no legal reason to retain the data, you must comply and confirm.

options.label: Yes — an erasure process exists and is documented
Partially — erasure happens on request but without a formal process
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a visible, accessible grievance mechanism for individuals to raise complaints or questions about their data?
questions.why: Grievance Redressal is a mandatory requirement under the DPDP Act. There must be a named contact and a response process.

options.label: Yes — a named contact and process are communicated to beneficiaries
Partially — a contact exists but is not clearly communicated
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you respond to all data-related requests (access, correction, deletion, complaints) within your published timeline?
questions.why: Unresolved requests can be escalated to the Data Protection Board. Timely response — within 90 days — is required under DPDP Rules.

options.label: Yes — requests are tracked and resolved within timeline
Partially — most requests are resolved but tracking is informal
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Has your organisation assigned at least one person to be responsible for data protection — even if not a full-time role?
questions.why: Without ownership, no compliance work can proceed consistently. Someone must be accountable.

options.label: Yes — a named person with a defined mandate
Partially — someone is informally responsible but without a defined role
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Do you have a written privacy or data protection policy that explains what data you collect, why, and how you protect it?
questions.why: A written policy helps you stay consistent, builds trust with beneficiaries, and demonstrates accountability to regulators and funders.

options.label: Yes — a written policy exists and is shared with staff
Partially — a draft or partial policy exists
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Have your staff and volunteers received any training on what personal data is, why protecting it matters, and how to handle it carefully?
questions.why: The organisation is responsible for how staff handle data. Training reduces the risk of accidental breaches.

options.label: Yes — structured training has been conducted and documented
Partially — informal briefings have happened but no structured training
No / Not Sure

questions.text: If a personal data breach occurs, can you identify it, assess the impact, and notify affected individuals and the Data Protection Board when required?
questions.why: Breach notification to the Board is mandatory. Penalties are highest in breach situations. Detection and response speed matter.

options.label: Yes — a breach response plan exists and has been tested
Partially — we would respond but have no documented plan
No / Not Sure

questions.text: Have you conducted any form of privacy or data protection review — such as a self-assessment, audit, or gap analysis — in the last 12 months?
questions.why: Periodic review is a sign of accountability. Funders and regulators increasingly expect evidence of structured self-assessment.

options.label: Yes — a review has been completed and documented
Partially — an informal review has happened but nothing documented
No / Not Sure

glossary.definition: Any organisation or person who decides why and how personal data is collected and used. As an NGO collecting beneficiary data, your organisation is a Data Fiduciary.

A Data Fiduciary designated by the Government as processing data at very large scale or of a particularly sensitive nature. Subject to stricter obligations including mandatory Data Protection Officers and audits.
The individual whose personal data is being collected or processed — in an NGO context, this is typically your beneficiary, staff member, volunteer, or donor.
Any third party that handles personal data on your behalf under a contract — for example, your cloud storage provider, survey tool, or HR software vendor.
A registered intermediary through which individuals can centrally give, review, and withdraw consent for data processing. Rules on Consent Managers are yet to be fully notified.
Any data that directly or indirectly identifies an individual — including name, phone number, address, Aadhaar, location, photo, IP address, and more.
Categories of data requiring heightened protection — financial information, health and medical records, biometric data, sexual orientation, religious or political beliefs.
The requirement to store certain categories of personal data within India's geographical borders. Specific categories and rules are yet to be formally notified.
Any unauthorised access, disclosure, alteration, or destruction of personal data that could harm the individuals to whom it belongs.
A mandatory mechanism through which individuals can raise complaints or questions about how their data is being handled. Organisations must designate a named contact person.
Sharing or transferring personal data to an entity located in another country. The Government may restrict transfers to specific countries or for specific categories of data.
Personal data may only be used for the specific purpose for which it was collected and for which consent was obtained. Using data for a different purpose requires fresh consent.
An individual's right to have their personal data deleted when there is no longer a lawful reason to retain it. The organisation must confirm deletion when requested.
A designated person responsible for overseeing an organisation's compliance with the DPDP Act. Mandatory for Significant Data Fiduciaries; strongly recommended for all NGOs handling sensitive data.
